does: This paper demonstrates a critical vulnerability in SAE-based interventions for model safety. The authors show that clamping specific SAE features identified as harmful does not reliably prevent model misbehavior. They formulate post-intervention recovery as a constrained optimization problem in residual space, where they successfully recover suppressed behaviors by finding perturbations that maintain the intervened feature values while reconstructing the original harmful output. The attack succeeds even when the intervention remains active throughout optimization and generation, using encoder-orthogonal updates to ensure the recovered behavior is not simply undoing the clamping mechanism.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in using Sparse Autoencoders for mechanistic interpretability and safety applications, extending critiques of latent-space defenses similar to prior work on jailbreaking and adversarial robustness in language models. It engages with the growing literature on feature-based interventions and interpretability-based safety, which assume that identified features provide reliable control points. The paper responds to optimistic assumptions in defenses built on SAE decompositions, connecting to broader research on the limitations of mechanistic interpretability for alignment and the surprising difficulty of reliably suppressing model behaviors through targeted interventions.
enables: This finding opens important directions for improving SAE-based defenses by identifying why recovery is possible and what additional constraints or mechanisms could prevent it. It motivates research into whether multiple simultaneous features must be targeted, whether different SAE architectures are more robust, or whether post-intervention monitoring can detect recovery attempts. The work suggests that purely feature-based safety approaches may be insufficient and points toward hybrid defenses combining SAE interventions with other monitoring techniques. It also raises questions about the fundamental limits of using linear sparse decompositions for safety-critical control and whether non-linear or ensemble-based safety mechanisms could provide stronger guarantees.
